title: Rebound

jokes.text: David calling Alexis's rebound 'alarmingly manic and regressive' while clearly having been waiting up for her
jokes.setup: David is ostensibly worried about Alexis's emotional wellbeing after her breakup with Ted
jokes.punchline: His clinical, dramatic language ('alarmingly manic and regressive') reveals he's performing concern while actually being judgmental and nosy
jokes.explanation: Very David Rose — using overly formal psychological vocabulary to dress up petty nosiness as genuine concern. Character-specific and well-delivered but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, she's clearly not thinking straight. She's vulnerable right now, and this guy is taking advantage of that.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, you're being a little dark about this, don't you think?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm being realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're being the Grim Reaper. You walk into a room and flowers wilt.

jokes.text: David calling himself the 'Grim Reaper of romance'
jokes.setup: Johnny pushes back on David's doom-and-gloom take on Alexis's dating life
jokes.punchline: The label 'Grim Reaper of romance' is so apt and specific it becomes an instant character summary
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical insult that is both funny as a standalone line and perfectly encapsulates David's character. High quotability because it's vivid and transferable. Johnny's delivery of the question is what makes it land — he's not wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We were just talking about... you... ganda. Uganda. The country.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Uganda?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, it's a fascinating place. We were discussing its natural resources and political climate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Mmhmm, very pressing issues in Uganda.

jokes.text: David pivoting mid-sentence: 'We were just having the most stimulating conversation about you... ganda! Ugandan politics.'
jokes.setup: Alexis catches David and Johnny clearly talking about her; David scrambles to cover
jokes.punchline: The desperate pivot to 'Uganda' — barely rescued into 'Ugandan politics' — is so transparently bad it becomes the joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best gags. The mid-word pivot ('you...ganda!') is a brilliant piece of writing — specific enough to feel like a real panicked improvisation, absurd enough to be immediately funny. Perfectly David: he commits fully to an obviously terrible lie. High rewatch value because you catch the exact moment the lie forms.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What were you guys just talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Nothing! We were just... talking about the weather.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, very boring weather talk.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were literally just looking at me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, we weren't.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We were looking... past you. At the wall.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Were you guys waiting up for me?' / Johnny and David in overlapping denial: 'Ah! N-no! No, I actually didn't know that you had left.'
jokes.setup: Alexis walks in on two people obviously waiting up for her
jokes.punchline: Their flustered, overlapping, transparently false denials — especially Johnny's 'I didn't even know you had left' which makes no sense given David's opening monologue
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy of exposed lies. The internal contradiction in Johnny's denial (contradicting the conversation we just heard) rewards attentive viewers. Standard sitcom beat but executed cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, he took me to this incredible steakhouse, and I just... I assumed he was going to pay. Like, obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, it's just always been that way for me. Men pay. That's just how it works.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'it was like, way too expensive, but hey, I'm not the one paying.'
jokes.setup: Alexis describes her date at an expensive steakhouse
jokes.punchline: Her breezy, zero-guilt shrug at someone else paying the bill is pure Alexis — shallow but charming
jokes.explanation: Light Alexis character moment. Not particularly original — rich girl doesn't feel guilty about expensive dates is expected — but delivered with such economy and commitment it still gets a smile.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's the spirit! You need to get back out there and show the world what you're made of. You're a catch, darling. A real catch. So go forth and... reel them in.

jokes.text: David's clipped, incredulous repetition of '"Artie?"'
jokes.setup: Alexis casually mentions she's been on a date with someone named 'Artie'
jokes.punchline: David's single-word echo, dripping with judgment, says everything without saying anything
jokes.explanation: Peak economy. One word, maximum character. David's talent for expressing contempt through repetition is a running character signature. Not flashy but very true to the show's comedic voice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, well, if you need a nickname, I could call you 'Art.' You know, short for Artie.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great, Dave. Real creative. Hey, speaking of nicknames, how about I call you 'Clueless'? Short for David.

jokes.text: Moira's 'Fling forward!'
jokes.setup: Johnny tepidly suggests Alexis's rebound seems quick; Moira offers enthusiastic counterpoint
jokes.punchline: Her invented rallying cry 'Fling forward!' — a portmanteau of 'fling' and 'spring forward' — is signature Moira: theatrical, vaguely nonsensical, somehow confident
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Moira wordplay. The phrase is absurd, punny, and oddly motivational — only Moira Rose would invent a life philosophy in two words and deliver it with theatrical conviction. High quotability because it's the kind of thing you'd want to use in real life.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, he certainly seems like a man who respects personal boundaries and the concept of consent.

jokes.text: Artie showing up at the motel room looking for 'Lexi' — a man clearly much older than expected
jokes.setup: Alexis has been casually describing her fun new date; we haven't seen him yet
jokes.punchline: Artie appears at the door — visually much older than anyone anticipated from Alexis's breezy description — which recontextualizes everything
jokes.explanation: A purely visual reveal gag — the comedy comes entirely from the audience seeing Artie for the first time and understanding in an instant why everyone's been worried. The setup was expertly paced across the prior scene. Rewatch bonus because you see it coming on second viewing and enjoy the dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, there are a lot of variables that could affect the final quote.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Have you asked them what they want?

jokes.text: David: 'I'm her brother, David, but you can call me Davey.' Artie: 'Hey, how you doin', Davey?'
jokes.setup: David is clearly thrown by the sight of this older man; he awkwardly introduces himself
jokes.punchline: David's involuntary self-diminution ('Davey') — immediately exploited by Artie who calls him that without hesitation — is a perfect power-dynamic flip
jokes.explanation: David, who controls every interaction through carefully calibrated aloofness, is immediately destabilized into offering a nickname he would never use. Artie's cheerful acceptance is salt in the wound. Strong character-based comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why is she being so nice to me? It's actually quite disturbing.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Our Lexi is making some wonderful choices.' — delivered with barely concealed horror
jokes.setup: The family has just processed Artie's appearance
jokes.punchline: Moira's perfectly controlled understatement — saying the opposite of what she clearly means with impeccable composure — is the button on the entire cold open
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in comedic economy. Five words to end the cold open, saying everything through subtext. Moira's theatrical self-control making the understatement even funnier. Perfect button joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So I was just wondering if you might have any positions available at the store?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, we're not really hiring right now.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I really need a job. My family's going through some financial difficulties.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry to hear that, but—

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: My mother's sick. And my father left us. I don't have anywhere else to turn, David. You're my only hope.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I understand, but I really can't—

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I guess some people just don't care about the suffering of others.

jokes.text: David's passive-aggressive response about the florist quote: 'Like you calling the florist, and getting a quote? That might be one of them.'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks what 'variables' are in play for getting a floral quote
jokes.punchline: David's airy suggestion that 'calling the florist' is one of many complex variables — when it's obviously the only variable and obviously his job — punctures his own avoidance hilariously
jokes.explanation: Classic David-Patrick dynamic — Patrick's straight-man precision vs. David's elaborate evasion of simple tasks. Competent but familiar territory for the show at this point.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Have you considered working at a strip club? The tips are really good, and you get to meet so many interesting people.

jokes.text: Jocelyn waving at David and Patrick from across the room 'in a very disturbing way'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are having a quiet conversation at brunch
jokes.punchline: David's description of Jocelyn's friendly wave as 'disturbing' is both a visual gag (we can imagine the enthusiastic wave) and a character joke about David's aversion to small-town warmth
jokes.explanation: Light visual/character beat. David's 'disturbing' is a funny word choice for a friendly wave. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's a very clean establishment. They have a strict no-touching policy.

jokes.text: Jocelyn guilting David and Patrick: 'I'll be the mother with the baby at home looking for some extra cash, after I invested in your father's business. I'll be over here.'
jokes.setup: David has been vague and evasive about hiring Jocelyn; she sees through it
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's guilt-bomb — delivered with aggressive cheerfulness — is a masterful social maneuver. The callback to Roland's investment and then walking away mid-conversation is perfectly uncomfortable.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn weaponizing vulnerability with perfect social precision. The 'I'll be over here' walk-away is the kicker that makes the guilt inescapable. Strong character comedy with genuine cringe discomfort for David and Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? You're already wearing... that.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, that's a lot to unload over brunch.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has just walked away after dropping a comprehensive guilt bomb
jokes.punchline: David's crisp, horrified understatement perfectly captures the social ambush they just experienced
jokes.explanation: Short, sharp reaction line. Very David — cutting through social discomfort with a crisp summary. Economy is the strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he would have good stories. He's been alive for quite some time.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'There is an opening for a waitress at Bazongas Gentlemen's Club. I guess I could try that.'
jokes.setup: Patrick and David are being reluctant about offering Jocelyn a job; she escalates her guilt trip
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn threatening to waitress at a strip club — delivered with the same cheerful matter-of-factness as everything else she says — is a magnificently absurd escalation
jokes.explanation: The name 'Bazongas Gentlemen's Club' alone is worth significant points — it's exactly the kind of small-town strip club name that's both believable and inherently funny. Jocelyn's cheerful willingness to go there as a genuine alternative is perfect character comedy. One of the episode's stronger jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Artie is really into jazz. Like, really into it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

jokes.text: David: 'I don't know what's wrong with Bazongas.'
jokes.setup: Patrick scrambles to find a better solution than Jocelyn working at a strip club
jokes.punchline: David, petulantly refusing to offer Jocelyn the job, deadpans that he sees no problem with the Bazongas option — because it solves his problem
jokes.explanation: The callback lands harder than the original setup. David's genuine enthusiasm for the Bazongas option — purely out of selfish reluctance to hire Jocelyn — is both a great character moment and a perfectly timed one-liner. 'Bazongas' is inherently funny to say.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We're spiritually compatible. He's like, an old soul.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he's 73.

jokes.text: Jocelyn saying she'll go home to change into something 'more casual' — David's horrified reaction beat
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has just successfully guilted her way into a trial at the Apothecary; she leaves to change
jokes.punchline: Her announcement that what she's currently wearing isn't 'casual enough' for the store — implying something even more alarming is coming — lands as a silent horror beat for David
jokes.explanation: Functional visual-adjacent beat. The humor comes from what we imagine Jocelyn might wear that's 'more casual' than what she already has on, and David's invisible horror at that prospect.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking of taking a casual weekend trip.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That sounds nice. Where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's to meet someone's family.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, how serious is this?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Very serious. I'm meeting his entire family, including his grandchildren.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Maybe having Jocelyn on staff will give a little extra time to call the florist.'
jokes.setup: Patrick tries to find a silver lining in the Jocelyn situation for David
jokes.punchline: The callback to the florist task David has been avoiding all episode — Patrick's subtle way of saying 'you created this problem by procrastinating' — is a perfectly timed passive-aggressive move
jokes.explanation: Elegant callback that serves double duty: it's funny as a standalone zinger and it reminds us of the earlier joke while deepening the Patrick/David dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is someone actually calling me? Like, on the phone? Like a psychopath?

jokes.text: Johnny asking if Alexis knows what 'scatting' is — Alexis: 'Do you know what scatting is?' Johnny: 'I do, not a huge fan.'
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions Artie loves to 'scat along' at the jazz festival
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat 'I do, not a huge fan' — delivered as if scatting is a personal affront rather than a jazz tradition — is a perfectly dry dad joke
jokes.explanation: A small perfect joke. Johnny's dismissive 'I do, not a huge fan' implies he's given scatting serious consideration and found it wanting — the certainty is what makes it funny. Economy is the real strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, you don't need to yell into the phone. Yes, I can hear you. No, the phone doesn't need to be plugged in. Well, it does eventually, but not right now.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'If you were guessing, Alexis, uh... how old a soul would you say Arthur is?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been describing Artie as an 'old soul' like herself; Johnny has been building toward asking his real question
jokes.punchline: The euphemism 'old soul' used to gently probe Artie's literal age is both a clever construction and perfect dad-diplomacy
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of Alexis's own phrase turned against her. Johnny using 'old soul' as a Trojan horse to ask about literal age is smart writing — it's simultaneously diplomatic and devastating. Character-specific: only Johnny Rose would approach this with this much careful indirection.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Hi! Welcome! Can I help you find anything today?

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm looking for something in a medium.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, a medium! That's actually the size my gynecologist said my uterus is. Medium-sized. I have a tilted uterus, which is why I can't use tampons, so I have to use pads, and let me tell you, the chafing in the summer is just—well, it's unbearable.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'his kids and grandkids are gonna be there' — the casual escalation of Artie's family situation
jokes.setup: Alexis has been describing a 'casual fun' weekend trip to wine country with Artie
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Artie's 'ex-wife owns a vineyard' and 'kids and grandkids' will be there completely demolishes her claim that this is casual — without her realizing it
jokes.explanation: The escalation is perfectly structured — each new detail (wine country trip → ex-wife's vineyard → kids AND grandkids) lands as a separate gut punch. Alexis's obliviousness to how this undermines her 'totally casual' framing is excellent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You can also use it as a personal lubricant, a hair gel, or a cooking oil.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's very versatile.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, it's sounding uh, less and less casual by the minute.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just described meeting Artie's grandchildren at his ex-wife's vineyard for his birthday
jokes.punchline: Johnny's masterful understatement cuts to the heart of the absurdity with perfect economy
jokes.explanation: A reliable joke type for this show — the measured, understated response to escalating absurdity. Eugene Levy delivers these perfectly and the writing gives him exactly the right number of words.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about getting into real estate, and I just sold a house in like five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's great, Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yeah, it really makes me wonder how you and Patrick have been in this business for so long and haven't figured it out yet.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Oh my God, is he calling me right now?! That is so 2001.'
jokes.setup: Artie apparently calls Alexis on the phone rather than texting
jokes.punchline: Alexis's horror at receiving an actual phone call — treating it as a relic of the early 2000s — is both generational comedy and Alexis-specific character detail
jokes.explanation: 'That is so 2001' is perfectly Alexis — specific, slightly wrong (phone calls were obviously normal in 2001, her objection is really about the cultural shift away from them), and delivered with genuine indignation. Strong quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: How about 'Rose Apothecary: We're here to help!'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... that's actually pretty good, Jocelyn.

jokes.text: Alexis picking up the call: 'Hey, babe. Yep, you just double-tap on photo with your finger.'
jokes.setup: Artie has called Alexis, apparently for tech support
jokes.punchline: The overheard one-sided conversation — Alexis explaining how to double-tap a photo to an elderly man — perfectly illustrates the generational gap without needing to say it explicitly
jokes.explanation: Show-don't-tell comedy at its best. We don't need to see Artie; Alexis's patient explanation of basic smartphone functions tells us everything about their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've been assailed. Positively besieged by what appears to be avian projectiles. My car looks like it's been through some sort of poultry-based apocalypse. And I smell like a breakfast buffet that's been left out in the sun.

jokes.text: Jocelyn's unsolicited advice about body milk and diaper rash, completely ignoring David's 'let them wander' instructions
jokes.setup: David has just finished explaining his 'let customers wander, don't hard-sell' philosophy to Jocelyn
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn immediately, enthusiastically, and completely ignores the instruction — using extremely personal oversharing about her own post-baby body and stretch marks to sell a product — and it works perfectly
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured irony: the character who 'doesn't belong' here immediately outperforms through the exact approach she was told not to use. Jocelyn oversharing about stretch marks and diaper rash to sell luxury body milk is both funny and reveals something true about different approaches to connection.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Or maybe it's about David.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Have a "Rose-y" day!'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has just made her first sale at the Apothecary
jokes.punchline: Her invented sign-off — 'Rose-y day' (combining the store name with 'rosy') — is exactly the kind of enthusiastic, slightly-too-cute small-town cheerfulness that is antithetical to the store's brand
jokes.explanation: The pun is genuinely cute, which is exactly why it's wrong for Rose Apothecary. Jocelyn's pride in it vs. David's quiet horror is a perfect encapsulation of their relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it is always about me, David. I am a woman of a certain notoriety. When I walk down the street, people point and stare. I cannot simply pop into a grocery store without causing a minor disturbance. Fame is a burden, darling, and one that I have carried with grace and poise for decades.

jokes.text: David: 'We won't be doing "Rose-y day."' / Jocelyn: 'No?' / David: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn delivers 'Have a Rose-y day!' with tremendous enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: David's flat, immediate, two-word veto — and Jocelyn's innocent 'No?' — is the perfect button on the scene
jokes.explanation: The exchange is three words total and contains an entire relationship. Economy at its finest. David's 'No' with no elaboration is as funny as anything else in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, fame is a very dangerous thing. I once knew a woman who appeared on the cover of a soap opera digest, and within weeks, she was recognized at a Piggly Wiggly in Ohio. The stress was unbearable.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Either a flock of poultry has delivered its ova mid-flight upon our car, or I've become the victim of a vandalization!'
jokes.setup: Moira discovers her car has been egged
jokes.punchline: Her ornate, elaborate phrasing for 'someone threw eggs at the car' — treating it as either a natural bird phenomenon or a criminal assault — is peak Moira linguistic excess
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Moira lines. 'Delivered its ova mid-flight' for 'laid an egg on' is a stunning circumlocution that only Moira Rose would use. The two-option framing (bird accident OR criminal vandalization) is also funny — as if those are the only two possibilities. The line is long but earns every word through character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We need to take every precaution. Board up the windows, install motion sensors, and for God's sake, someone get me a suit of armor.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, it's just egg.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Just egg? JUST EGG? Do you know what egg does to a finish, David?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ruins it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ruins it. We're essentially under siege.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Um... you sure it's for you, Mrs. Rose? Because that's a family car, and I can think of a handful of people who'd wanna egg David.'
jokes.setup: Moira assumes she's the target of egg-based vandalism due to her fame
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry, reasonable suggestion that David might be the actual target — with the added detail that she can think of 'a handful' of people who'd want to do it — is perfect Stevie deadpan
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout jokes. The comedic logic is airtight: of course David has more egg-worthy enemies than Moira. The 'handful of people' qualifier — implying Stevie has actually mentally catalogued David's enemies — is the killer detail. Completely character-specific to Stevie's dry wit and her intimate familiarity with the family's chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course he's the target. Do you know what it's like to be married to someone of my stature? The constant scrutiny, the relentless attention, the burden of being photographed at one's worst angles. I didn't ask for this life of notoriety, and yet here I am, bearing the weight of recognition with nothing but my grace and my bone structure to sustain me.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You are blessed with anonymity, and thus will never have to know the crippling fear that accompanies global repute.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has reasonably suggested Moira may not be the target
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses Stevie's logic by essentially calling her a nobody — but framing it as a blessing Stevie can't fully appreciate — is both delusional and bizarrely touching
jokes.explanation: Moira at her most sublimely self-deluded. The phrasing is so ornate and the condescension so gentle that it's almost kind — which makes it funnier. 'Global repute' for 'being a soap opera actress' is delicious.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: So I'm at this restaurant, right? And the host says, 'We don't serve lunch or dinner, we serve lunner.' I said, 'What the hell is lunner?'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's not a real word.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's what I said! But apparently it's the meal between lunch and dinner, so it's lunner.

jokes.text: Moira: 'After my first centerfold with Soap Opera Digest, a deranged fan deposited locks of hair into my glove compartment.'
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining to Stevie why her fame makes her a target for vandalism
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'Soap Opera Digest' centerfold (not exactly Playboy) combined with 'locks of hair in the glove compartment' as the defining fan-worship story is magnificently absurd
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'Soap Opera Digest centerfold' — not a prestigious publication, but Moira treats it with the gravity of a Vogue cover — is perfect. The hair-in-glove-compartment detail is specific enough to feel real and absurd enough to be hilarious. This is the kind of throwaway backstory line that makes Schitt's Creek writers exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, you can't be drinking mimosas at this hour. What will people think?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, mimosas are literally just orange juice. It's basically breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not the point. It's about propriety and—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're right. I should wait until it's a more appropriate time, like noon.

jokes.text: Moira: 'my return to prominence has painted another giant bulls-eye on my thorax!'
jokes.setup: Moira is building her case for why she specifically was targeted
jokes.punchline: Using 'thorax' instead of 'back' or 'chest' — a word that sounds clinical and insectile — for a dramatic declaration about being a target is pure Moira
jokes.explanation: Another Moira word-choice joke — 'thorax' is wonderfully wrong, specific, and on-brand. It's funny that she chooses the most anatomically precise and least emotionally resonant body-part word for a dramatic statement.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not your dad.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Um, I could give you this bucket of soap.' / Moira: 'No! You never tamper with a crime scene.'
jokes.setup: Moira has declared the egged car a crime scene requiring council intervention
jokes.punchline: Stevie's practical offer of soap vs. Moira's outraged refusal — treating a few broken eggs like a murder scene — is a perfect collision of their worldviews
jokes.explanation: The straight-man/absurdist pairing working exactly as it should. Stevie's practicality makes Moira's drama funnier; Moira's drama makes Stevie's practicality funnier. 'You never tamper with a crime scene' for egg cleanup is a great line.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, Santa gave me a lighter when I was a baby.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'll have to go to council. Get in please, Stevie. As of now, you are my star witness. And sit low, no point endangering both our lives.'
jokes.setup: Moira has decided to treat this as a criminal matter
jokes.punchline: Designating Stevie a 'star witness' and telling her to 'sit low' — as if they're fleeing a crime scene rather than driving to a town hall — takes the absurdity to its logical extreme
jokes.explanation: 'Star witness' and 'sit low' as safety precautions against an egg-related conspiracy is pleasingly committed absurdism. The escalation of paranoia is well-paced.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's not an insult, that's just a factual statement about my age.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But if you're going to call me 'old man' instead of 'Dad,' then I'm going to start calling you 'disappointment' instead of 'son.'

jokes.text: Johnny at the café, hearing someone recount a story about asking for a 'brunch menu' and being given a 'lunner menu'
jokes.setup: Johnny has arrived at the café and overheard a conversation mid-stream
jokes.punchline: The portmanteau 'lunner' (lunch + dinner) as a menu category at the Café Tropical is quintessentially Twyla-town absurdist small-town detail
jokes.explanation: A nice small-town absurdism joke. 'Lunner' is a funny word and the café giving it its own menu feels right for this world. Not the episode's strongest but adds texture.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: What do you do, Artie?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a semi-retired textile importer. I own a modest home in the suburbs, drive a sensible sedan, and I take my family on one nice vacation every other year.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'd say I'm living the dream.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, don't worry, I'm over 21, Dad.' — said to Artie about her mimosa
jokes.setup: Artie makes a comment about there being 'enough sugar in mimosas' for his blood sugar, which Johnny echoes with concern
jokes.punchline: Alexis mishears the concern as being about her age rather than Artie's medical need — revealing she's not tracking the conversation at all
jokes.explanation: Light Alexis character moment — her self-centeredness causes her to completely miss the actual concern. Functional but not particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know, being a parent is about guiding your children, showing them the way...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, perhaps you should focus on that before Alexis makes you a grandfather.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, please don't call me "Dad."' — to Artie
jokes.setup: Alexis sarcastically tells Artie he can call her father 'Dad'; Johnny had already asked Artie not to call him 'Dad'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's mortified request — please don't call him 'Dad' when Artie is clearly closer to his age — is a perfect button on the generational absurdity
jokes.explanation: Six words, maximum discomfort. The fact that Johnny has to specify this implies it could be a real possibility — and Artie's approximate age means the word 'Dad' carries a different and more disturbing weight than usual. Eugene Levy's delivery would make this land perfectly. Strong joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You said, 'Roland Schitt is a man of very limited means and even more limited faculties.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I said 'faculties,' not 'facilities.' There's a difference.

jokes.text: Twyla casually dropping: 'No, but I think he dated my aunt. I don't know, I was a baby at the time, but there's a photo of him holding me at Christmas.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if Twyla knows anything about Artie
jokes.punchline: Twyla offhandedly reveals Artie dated her aunt when Twyla was a baby — and there's photographic evidence — delivered with complete casualness as though this is entirely normal
jokes.explanation: This is peak Twyla: a piece of personal backstory so specific and so casually delivered that it functions as an instant character study. The detail that there's a Christmas photo of baby Twyla being held by a man who is now dating Alexis is genuinely surreal — and the 'I was a baby at the time' qualifier (as if that explains why she doesn't know him) is perfect. One of the episode's best jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This PR crisis is the worst thing that's ever happened to this town.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It makes the time I wore the same suit two days in a row look like nothing.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'And I remember that, because that was the year Santa brought me a lighter.'
jokes.setup: Twyla is explaining how she knows about the Christmas photo
jokes.punchline: The reason she remembers that Christmas is because Santa brought her a lighter — a detail so dark, so specific, and so casually stated that it becomes an entire childhood in one sentence
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best in the series. 'Santa brought me a lighter' is eleven words that contain an entire dark comedy origin story. It's funny on first hearing, it's funnier when you process it, and it's funniest when you think about Twyla's parents wrapping a lighter for a baby. The casual delivery — as a throwaway clarification — is what elevates it to legendary status. This is the kind of joke that gets clipped and quoted. Fully earned, completely character-specific, maximum economy. Approaches the top of the calibration scale.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this wheelchair ramp situation, and I think we should call it 'Rampgate.'

jokes.text: Johnny (aside, after Artie calls him 'old man'): 'That's a smidge worse than "Dad."'
jokes.setup: Artie has called Johnny 'old man' instead of 'Dad'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's internal monologue aside — 'a smidge worse than Dad' — is a perfect calibration joke, ranking insults against each other with total seriousness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback. The ranking of 'old man' vs. 'Dad' as insults — with the precise word 'smidge' — is exactly the kind of specificity that elevates a good joke to great. 'Smidge' is doing enormous work: it implies Johnny has given serious thought to this hierarchy of humiliation. Among the episode's very best.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was simply being complimentary. I said she had wonderful bone structure for someone of her... stature.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And I meant it as a genuine compliment that she managed to maintain such a... trim silhouette despite her obvious love of carbohydrates.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was merely pointing out that for someone with her particular genetic disadvantages, she presented herself quite admirably.

jokes.text: Artie: 'I own a beautiful three-bedroom home with an above ground pool, if that's what you're worried about.'
jokes.setup: Artie has mentioned he used to own trailer parks, then quickly clarifies he doesn't live in a trailer
jokes.punchline: His unprompted defensive specification of 'above ground pool' — as if this is the gold standard of success — is both socioeconomically funny and reveals Artie's own class anxieties
jokes.explanation: The above-ground pool detail is perfect class comedy. To Artie it's a point of pride; to the Roses (who owned multiple properties) it reads very differently. The specificity — not just a pool, but specifically above-ground — is what makes it sing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I want to apologize to this town. You've all been so welcoming, and I realize I haven't always been... gracious in return.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This town may not have the sophistication of where I come from, but it has a certain... rustic charm. Like a quaint little dollhouse, if the dollhouse smelled like manure and desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And you people! So simple, yet somehow managing to function despite your obvious limitations. It's really quite inspiring.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'No, that wasn't my primary concern, Arthur.'
jokes.setup: Artie thinks Johnny is worried about his housing situation
jokes.punchline: Johnny's polite but definitive correction — 'that wasn't my primary concern' — implies there are bigger worries, delivered with perfect tact
jokes.explanation: Functional Johnny understatement. The 'primary concern' phrasing is diplomatically hilarious — it implies there are many concerns, just ranked differently. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I make a solemn vow to make things right with this town.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Now, who among you will clean my car?

jokes.text: Artie: 'I know it isn't always easy being a dad. And being a grandad's even harder.' — said to Johnny, who has no grandchildren
jokes.setup: Artie is trying to bond with Johnny over the difficulties of parenting
jokes.punchline: Artie's pivot to 'being a grandad's even harder' — to a man who is currently worried that his daughter is dating someone old enough to be her grandfather — is a beautiful piece of unintentional cruelty
jokes.explanation: A masterfully layered joke. Artie is genuinely trying to connect. But he's connecting over grandfather-hood with a man whose main concern is that his daughter might have a grandfather-figure as a boyfriend. The irony is entirely situational and completely earned by the narrative setup.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She's experiencing what I like to call 'beginner's luck.' It's a very real phenomenon.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's wonderful. She's a natural.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Or she's just won the equivalent of pulling the lever on a Casablanca slot machine.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Hello, council people! Why, you ask, are we here, trembling before you?' — not trembling at all, completely theatrical
jokes.setup: Moira arrives at the town council to report her car being egged
jokes.punchline: Her performative, theatrical opening — 'trembling before you' while clearly not trembling — frames a minor neighborhood complaint as a dramatic audience with authority
jokes.explanation: Moira treating a council meeting like a stage performance is well-established character territory, but executed with her characteristic excess. 'Trembling before you' while very clearly not trembling is a nice ironic touch.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You've been to a casino?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Patrick. I've been to a casino.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: And you lost money at the blackjack table?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: While wearing a blazer that cost more than my car?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was a very nice blazer, Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm not doubting the blazer. I'm just trying to figure out how you managed to have money to lose.

jokes.text: Stevie describing the carnage: 'Um, well, there were a couple of broken eggs on Mrs. Rose's car, and one on the back windshield.' / Roland: 'The windshield?' / Moira: 'The egg! That's the bestial level of attack with which we are dealing.'
jokes.setup: Moira has asked Stevie to 'describe the carnage' of the egg attack
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deliberately deflating description vs. Moira's insistence that one broken egg on a windshield represents 'bestial' violence — the collision between Stevie's matter-of-factness and Moira's drama is the joke
jokes.explanation: The three-way exchange is elegantly structured: Stevie deflates, Roland clarifies, Moira escalates wildly. 'Bestial level of attack' for one egg on a windshield is a great Moira line. The misunderstanding between 'the windshield' and 'the egg' is a tight little comedy beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He didn't know who Cher was.

jokes.text: Council member: 'Well, you know, after this little interview in People Magazine, I'm surprised those eggs weren't actually thrown at you.'
jokes.setup: Moira expects the council to be sympathetic; it turns out there's a People Magazine interview complicating things
jokes.punchline: The council member casually expresses that Moira deserved more eggs — delivered as a reasonable position — subverting the expected sympathy
jokes.explanation: The subversion of sympathetic council expectation is clean. 'I'm surprised those eggs weren't thrown at you' is a satisfying puncture of Moira's victimhood narrative.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Cat Stevens? More like Cat has-beens.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, actually, Cat Stevens is a very talented musician. He had some wonderful hits in the '70s.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, nobody cares about Cat Stevens.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not true. Morning Has Broken is a beautiful song.

jokes.text: Moira's correction: 'Oh no, I described the town as the last place I'd ever want to end up.' — as if the distinction matters
jokes.setup: The council reads Moira's quote that she called the town 'the last place you'd ever wanna end up'
jokes.punchline: Moira's pedantic correction — changing 'you' to 'I' as if that makes the insult meaningfully less offensive — is perfectly in character and completely absurd
jokes.explanation: Exquisitely Moira. The correction is technically accurate and completely misses the point. Only Moira Rose would think that changing the pronoun transforms an insult into something defensible. Character-specific and smartly written.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, they're very... efficient. You know, minimal square footage means minimal heating costs in the winter.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And the sense of community is actually quite lovely. Everyone knows their neighbors.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Plus, there's a certain rustic charm to the open-air living concept.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And the trailer itself is mobile, which is really quite innovative when you think about it.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Um, since I'm no longer the star witness in this criminal case, can I get back to work?'
jokes.setup: The council meeting has devolved; Moira's case has fallen apart
jokes.punchline: Stevie's request to be released from 'star witness' duty — dryly accepting Moira's framing while clearly not believing it — is a perfect Stevie moment
jokes.explanation: Good callback — Stevie playing along with 'star witness' terminology while clearly considering this the most absurd use of her morning. Her deadpan bureaucratic request is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I should have protected you more.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I dated like half the cast of 'Fast Five.'

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, you see here, I'm exaggerating for effect.'
jokes.setup: The council pushes back on Moira's dramatic claims about the egg attack
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual admission that she was 'exaggerating for effect' — as if this is a completely normal and defensible thing to do in front of the town council — is a moment of accidental self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Moira briefly and accidentally becoming self-aware — admitting dramatic exaggeration as though it's a legitimate rhetorical strategy — is a nice subversion of her usual total obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I appreciate the offer, but it's just not my aesthetic, and I'm not going to compromise my integrity for a position that doesn't align with my values.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? Did you just... No. I don't talk like that.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You literally just said something very similar yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but when I say it, it's different.

jokes.text: Roland: 'this could be the biggest scandal since we installed the church wheelchair ramp backwards'
jokes.setup: Roland is explaining the severity of the tourism PR problem Moira has caused
jokes.punchline: The 'backwards wheelchair ramp' as the town's previous biggest scandal is a perfectly absurd small-town detail — the specificity (backwards ramp at a church) makes it funnier than a generic scandal
jokes.explanation: Classic small-town world-building joke. The backwards wheelchair ramp is specific enough to be real and absurd enough to be funny. 'Ramp-gate' (the later reference) extends it well.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You can keep the sales bell. I don't really need it anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I figured you two could probably use it more than I do.

jokes.text: Roland: 'we can't afford another "ramp-gate"'
jokes.setup: Roland is warning Moira about the PR consequences
jokes.punchline: Applying the '-gate' Watergate scandal suffix to the wheelchair ramp incident — treating it with the gravity of a political scandal — is a funny callback that deepens the joke
jokes.explanation: A standard '-gate' suffix joke but applied with enough specificity to work. 'Ramp-gate' is inherently funny. The callback adds value but the joke itself is familiar territory.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you have any quail? Or perhaps some foie gras?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: We have tuna salad.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Tuna salad? Twyla, I don't eat anything that swims in a can.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: It's fresh tuna.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, do you have anything with edible gold leaf? Or at least some microgreens?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: We have iceberg lettuce.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Iceberg? That's not even a real vegetable, it's a water delivery system. I'll just have a sparkling water. Make sure it's imported.

jokes.text: The crowd showing up to protest Moira with signs — 'Ooh, I think they're here for you, Moira!'
jokes.setup: Moira arrived at the council to report her victimization; now a crowd has formed outside
jokes.punchline: Roland's cheerful announcement that the protest is for Moira — turning her from victim to villain in the same scene — is a satisfying reversal
jokes.explanation: Functional narrative reversal joke. The visual of protestors holding signs outside a small-town council meeting over an egg-and-magazine-article incident is inherently absurd. Roland's enthusiasm is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they're clearly so popular, they've run out of everything. It's a testament to their success. We should film that.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Hey uh, folks, I can't quite read those signs, do you mind holding them up a little higher for me, there?'
jokes.setup: An angry crowd with protest signs has arrived for Moira
jokes.punchline: Rather than being intimidated, Moira asks the protesters to helpfully reposition their signs for her reading comfort — treating her own protest as a service event
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully Moira moment — her total social obliviousness to the hostility of the crowd, treating them as assistants. 'Do you mind holding them up a little higher for me, there?' has the cadence of someone asking for menus to be brought closer. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This apothecary is a peaceful sanctuary, where one can browse in complete and utter solitude.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, there are literally customers here right now.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, please. I'm trying to convey an atmosphere of zen-like emptiness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But it's not empty. There are people shopping.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they're not supposed to be here during filming.

jokes.text: Moira outside to protestors: 'I was assailed this morning... but the magazine neglected to include my entire quote. I specifically commended your ever-beating heart, and your elephantine generosity.'
jokes.setup: Moira is attempting to appease the protestors
jokes.punchline: 'Elephantine generosity' — a word that means large like an elephant — as a compliment is a typically Moira Freudian slip: technically complimentary, faintly insulting. Her claim that she 'specifically commended' these things also implies she was very specific about what was wrong with the town.
jokes.explanation: 'Elephantine generosity' is a perfectly chosen Moira compliment — she would use a word associated with enormous size and feel she was being poetic, not insulting. The audience will catch it even if the townspeople don't.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can you please stop saying that? Every time you say it, a customer doesn't come in.

jokes.text: Moira: '"A slice of heaven," is what I recall myself saying!'
jokes.setup: Moira is insisting her quote was taken out of context
jokes.punchline: Her confidence that she 'recalls saying' something she clearly never said — and the formulation 'I recall myself saying' (rather than 'I said') — implies she has a constructed memory of her own best intentions
jokes.explanation: The specific phrasing 'I recall myself saying' is doing a lot of work — she's confabulating a better version of events in real time and presenting it as memory. Very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think what makes Schitt's Creek special is the sense of community and the genuine warmth of the people who live here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Please know that I believe, deep down, there are worse places to be. Like in your shoes right now.'
jokes.setup: Moira is delivering what is supposed to be an apology/peace offering to the protestors
jokes.punchline: Her 'worse places to be... like in your shoes right now' is ostensibly an attempt at connection but functions as a subtle insult — she's saying she feels sorry for them for being protestors, not for anything she did
jokes.explanation: Moira attempting to apologize and accidentally insulting everyone even more deeply is her richest vein of comedy. 'There are worse places to be — like in your shoes right now' is sublimely layered: she thinks she's being humble and empathetic; she is in fact telling the protestors their lives are worse than hers. Character-perfect, structurally tight, highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Schitt's Creek: where the name may disappoint, but the experience won't.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I do have a few headshots in the boot of my car.'
jokes.setup: Roland has suggested Moira needs to fix the PR situation; she thinks a meet-and-greet might help
jokes.punchline: Moira keeping headshots in her car ('boot' = British term for trunk) as a standard PR resource — at a moment where her presence is making things worse — is the comedy of a person with no situational awareness
jokes.explanation: The headshots-in-the-boot detail is pure Moira worldbuilding. Of course she has them. The word 'boot' is also a nice character detail — her British-isms are themselves characterful.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I don't think you need more people knowing which car is yours, Moira.'
jokes.setup: Moira suggests a meet-and-greet from her car with headshots
jokes.punchline: Roland's practical objection — which is that her car is the one covered in eggs from people who already know which car is hers — is a beautifully dry reality check
jokes.explanation: A quiet Roland joke — his practical wisdom cutting through Moira's bad idea with one observation. The callback to the egged car is efficient and the logic is funny because it's completely sound.

jokes.text: Moira's closing vow to the crowd: 'I solemnly vow to make right by all of you. As soon as one of you details my car.'
jokes.setup: Moira is delivering a solemn public vow to repair relations with the town
jokes.punchline: The pivot from solemn community commitment to demanding someone clean her car — treating it as a fair exchange — is a perfect Moira button
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The structural whiplash — solemn communal vow → casual personal demand — is perfectly executed. 'Details my car' as a condition of social reconciliation is an ideal Moira joke: she genuinely doesn't see the problem. The line is completely quotable and earns the full scene that preceded it. This is exceptional character comedy.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I don't wanna rub salt in the wound here, David, but Jocelyn is kinda crushing it.'
jokes.setup: David has been standing in the corner giving Jocelyn 'cut-eye' all day instead of acknowledging her success
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'I don't wanna rub salt in the wound' while very clearly rubbing salt in the wound is a gentle but perfectly aimed dig
jokes.explanation: A well-worn phrase ('don't wanna rub salt in the wound') used in a context where it still gets mild comedy from the obvious lie of the framing. Functional but not particularly original.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm mentoring her, and monitoring her progress. And frankly, it's not moving as fast as I'd like.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has noted David is standing in the corner giving Jocelyn cut-eye
jokes.punchline: David reframing his petty jealousy and passive hostility as 'mentoring and monitoring' — and then claiming the mentorship isn't progressing fast enough — is a perfect David self-delusion joke
jokes.explanation: Exceptional David self-deception. He's genuinely convinced himself he's a mentor. 'It's not moving as fast as I'd like' — about the progress of his own sabotage — is the perfectly absurd button. The alliteration of 'mentoring and monitoring' is an accidental grace note.

jokes.text: David's 'Basic Instinct' slot machine casino analogy for Jocelyn's beginner's luck
jokes.setup: Patrick says Jocelyn's sales are real regardless of who the customers are
jokes.punchline: David's analogy — walking into a casino and winning big on your first pull of the 'Basic Instinct' slot machine — is a wonderfully specific piece of nonsense. The specificity of 'Basic Instinct' as a named slot machine reveals a very particular knowledge base.
jokes.explanation: The 'Basic Instinct' slot machine is a perfect David detail — specific, slightly off-brand for the analogy he's making, and immediately visual. It implies he has spent time in the kind of casino that has Basic Instinct-themed slot machines, which tells you a lot about David Rose's life before Schitt's Creek.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Okay, I'm beginning to think that that is the high point of your entire life.'
jokes.setup: David mentions his 'Basic Instinct' slot machine win as a reference point
jokes.punchline: Patrick's dry assessment that the slot machine moment may be David's peak life achievement — delivered with gentle concern rather than cruelty — is a perfect Patrick beat
jokes.explanation: Patrick's callback lands slightly harder than David's setup. The framing — not as a joke but as a sincere concern — makes it funnier. Patrick has clearly considered the trajectory of David's life and found the casino win to be the apex.

jokes.text: David: 'I had an ex in San Francisco who was a real estate agent, and basically made all of our friends buy penthouses just to make it look like he was doing well.'
jokes.setup: David is arguing that Jocelyn's customers might just be friends propping up her sales numbers
jokes.punchline: David's casual backstory reveal — that an ex of his manipulated their entire social circle into buying penthouses for his career optics — is hilariously specific and reveals volumes about his pre-Schitt's Creek social world
jokes.explanation: Another great David backstory joke. The specificity — San Francisco, real estate agent, penthouses — is exactly right. This casual revelation of a deeply dysfunctional relationship used as a totally normal reference point is peak David Rose.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah, but is that what we're really about? Like, just selling products?' / Patrick: 'Yep, that is literally the purpose of our store.'
jokes.setup: David is resisting admitting Jocelyn's sales are valid
jokes.punchline: David's existential reframing of the purpose of a store — treating 'selling products' as a possibly reductive mission — vs. Patrick's flat confirmation that yes, that is in fact the purpose
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured two-hander. David's brand-anxious existentialism ('is that what we're really about?') vs. Patrick's cheerful pragmatism ('that is literally the purpose of our store') is the essence of their dynamic. Patrick's 'literally' is the kill shot.

jokes.text: Jocelyn rings a bell — 'Another sale, boys!' / David: 'Where'd she get that bell?' / Patrick: 'She brought it from home.'
jokes.setup: David has been reluctantly conceding that Jocelyn is doing well
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn has apparently brought a bell from home specifically to announce her sales — which implies she planned this level of enthusiasm — and Patrick's deadpan 'she brought it from home' is the perfect explanation
jokes.explanation: An excellent visual/character comedy beat. The bell itself is funny. Patrick's explanation that she 'brought it from home' is devastating — it means Jocelyn anticipated her success enough to bring celebration equipment on day one of a trial run. The economy of 'She brought it from home' as the complete, perfect explanation is outstanding.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'who even is Cat Stevens?!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is supposedly heartbroken about the breakup; she mentions Artie's 'weird' makeout music
jokes.punchline: Cat Stevens as incomprehensible makeout music — and Alexis's genuine indignation at a musician she doesn't recognize — is both a generational joke and an Alexis character moment
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Cat Stevens as the incomprehensible makeout music is very funny — it's an ideal choice. Not anyone famous enough to be scandalous, not obscure enough to be hip. Just... Cat Stevens. Alexis's complete non-recognition is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I'm getting broken up with by gross old men!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is processing her heartbreak and rebound failure simultaneously
jokes.punchline: The plural 'men' — implying this is a pattern, not a one-off — combined with 'gross old' is a dark self-assessment delivered in Alexis's characteristic breezy indignation
jokes.explanation: The plural is the joke — Alexis has enough experience with this specific situation to use the plural 'men.' Dark self-awareness delivered without any self-awareness. Good Alexis line.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh, honey, no, he-he-he wasn't that gross. No, I mean, he-he seemed... nice. He was responsible. He did run a successful mobile-home business.'
jokes.setup: Alexis calls Artie a 'gross old man'; Johnny scrambles to defend him — while also clearly knowing details he shouldn't
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flustered, over-specific defense of Artie — 'successful mobile-home business' being his highest compliment — inadvertently reveals he talked to Artie, which Alexis immediately catches
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent — the joke is in the trap Johnny walks into by being too specific. 'Successful mobile-home business' as the peak of his defense is funny both as a compliment (the bar is low) and as a detail that gives him away. Alexis catching it immediately is the perfect payoff.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'How did you know about that?' / Johnny: 'What? How did I — where did I hear that?' / Alexis: 'You talked to him!'
jokes.setup: Johnny has accidentally revealed knowledge of Artie's mobile-home business
jokes.punchline: Johnny's transparent, multi-stage panic — the 'What? How did I — where did I hear that?' cascade — is a perfect comedic exposure of a terrible liar
jokes.explanation: The cascade of bad cover-ups is effective — each attempt worse than the last. Johnny Rose is a fundamentally honest man who can't lie convincingly, which is a good character trait for comedy.

jokes.text: Where were you when I was dating half the cast of 'White Squall?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is apologizing for being overprotective and overzealous. Alexis is processing his confession about talking to Artie.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's offhand reference to having dated 'half the cast of White Squall' — a real 1996 Ridley Scott film — is a great absurdist Alexis backstory drop, implying a chaotic romantic history.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Half the cast of White Squall' is specific, unexpected, and perfectly Alexis — her backstory is always full of absurdly specific celebrity and cultural references. The joke works on multiple levels: it references a real but obscure film, it implies a wild past, and it lands in the middle of a sincere emotional scene, which makes the whiplash funnier. Justifies 8+ because the specificity and character integration are exceptional.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Where were you when I was dating half the cast of "White Squall"?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has admitted he was trying to make up for not being there for Alexis during past heartbreaks
jokes.punchline: Alexis's throwaway reference to dating 'half the cast of White Squall' — a 1996 Ridley Scott maritime disaster film — is one of the most spectacularly specific Alexis backstory jokes in the series
jokes.explanation: Tied with the lighter joke as the episode's best. 'Half the cast of White Squall' is the perfect Alexis backstory reference — it's a real (obscure) film, the specific choice to say half (not all, not one) is funny, and the delivery as a completely casual memory is pitch-perfect. It implies a whole world of Alexis's chaotic youth that we don't see but completely believe. The show's writers at their absolute best: the joke is funny immediately, funnier when you know what White Squall is, and funniest when you think about what it means about Alexis's life.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, I don't really want to know.'
jokes.setup: Alexis invites Johnny to consider where he was when she was making terrible romantic choices as a youth
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat, immediate withdrawal — 'I don't really want to know' — after asking a question he then immediately regrets having raised, is perfect dad comedy
jokes.explanation: The perfect reaction to the White Squall revelation. Johnny opened the door and immediately decides to close it. The economy is perfect — five words that communicate the complete emotional reversal.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Okay. Yeah, cry for a bit.' — his parenting advice
jokes.setup: Alexis asks 'What am I gonna do now?' about her heartbreak
jokes.punchline: Johnny's parenting advice is simply 'cry for a bit' — practical, honest, and delivered with absolute matter-of-fact certainty
jokes.explanation: In a scene that's mostly emotional warmth, this lands as a perfect comedic button that's also genuinely good advice. The pause before it ('Okay. Yeah.') and the specificity of 'a bit' (not 'for a while,' not 'it out') is wonderful. Johnny's pragmatic dadness at its best.

jokes.text: Johnny staring at the door after the emotional scene: 'I wonder when David'll be home.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just had a tender moment with Alexis; she goes to cry
jokes.punchline: His immediate pivot to wondering about David — as if the emotional weight of the scene just made him miss his other child — is a very human and very funny beat
jokes.explanation: This quiet button joke is doing more than it looks like. Johnny's wondering about David after a tender Alexis moment suggests he's now worried about both of them, which is both comedic (anxious dad, immediately moving on) and quietly sweet.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'as David would say, I just don't think it's in my brand.' / David: 'I don't think I've ever said that.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has been offered the Rose Apothecary job and is turning it down
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn using David's brand-language to reject a job offer from David — and David reflexively denying he ever said it (he absolutely has) — is a perfect ironic reversal
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest structural jokes. Jocelyn weaponizing David's own brand vocabulary against him to reject his job offer is a beautifully constructed reversal. David's denial — 'I don't think I've ever said that' — when it's clearly completely in character for him, is the perfect final layer. The joke rewards anyone who's watched the series and knows David's tendency toward brand-speak.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'You guys can keep the bell. 'Cause you might need it a little more than me.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is leaving after declining the job offer; she had brought her own bell for sales announcements
jokes.punchline: Leaving the bell as charity — implying the store needs it to generate any excitement — is a beautifully executed parting shot disguised as kindness
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback to the bell joke, now weaponized. Jocelyn donating the bell to David and Patrick 'because you might need it more' is devastating — and the smile with which it's delivered makes it even better. The bell went from visual gag to symbol of Jocelyn's quiet dominance of this entire storyline.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'she does know this is a training session, right?' / David and Patrick's mutual confusion about what Jocelyn thinks just happened
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has left after declining the job offer and saying she'll pick up her paycheck tomorrow
jokes.punchline: The mutual realization that Jocelyn may think she's already been hired (and has a paycheck coming) vs. it being a trial — and the impossibility of the paycheck math — leaves David and Patrick stunned
jokes.explanation: A good button on the Jocelyn storyline. The paycheck detail implies Jocelyn has navigated this entire situation on her own terms and left with every advantage. David and Patrick's helpless confusion is earned.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'I'll come by tomorrow to pick up my paycheck.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has declined the job offer and said her goodbyes
jokes.punchline: On her way out, she mentions she'll return for a paycheck — for the trial day she apparently considers compensated — leaving David and Patrick stunned
jokes.explanation: A great button line — delivered as an afterthought, which is exactly the right register. The joke lives in the assumption: Jocelyn considers a trial day compensable without anyone agreeing to this. Her departing confidence is funnier than any elaboration would be.

jokes.text: David and Patrick: 'It's just not for her? / It's just not for her. / I got it. Thank you. / I think that's what it means.'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are trying to process Jocelyn's rejection of the job offer using her own words
jokes.punchline: Their circular, helpless repetition of Jocelyn's phrase back at each other — 'just not for her' — with decreasing comprehension, is a perfect absurdist button
jokes.explanation: The circular dialogue about 'not for her' is a nice structural joke — they're repeating it as if repetition will make it make sense, which it doesn't. The callback structure works well.

jokes.text: Moira filming a tourism video for the town: 'I'm film and television's Moira Rose. Won't you join me for a little stroll through the slice of paradise I like to call... the town where I currently am.'
jokes.setup: Moira has agreed to make a tourism video as her penance for the magazine interview
jokes.punchline: Her introduction — refusing to name the town and calling it only 'the town where I currently am' — is a spectacular piece of continued contempt disguised as compliance
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best moments. 'The town where I currently am' is a masterpiece of non-endorsement — she has technically described the town without ever saying anything positive or even naming it. The phrase implies she could leave at any moment and fully intends to. 'I'm film and television's Moira Rose' as an introduction is also perfect — the possessive 'film and television's' is a formulation nobody uses and is inherently funny.

jokes.text: Moira at the Café Tropical: 'I'll have the Miso Black Cod, please.' / Twyla: 'We don't have that.' / Moira: 'You mean, you're sold out, is more like it!'
jokes.setup: Moira is filming a tourism video at the café and orders an item not on the menu
jokes.punchline: Moira refusing to accept that a small-town diner simply doesn't have miso black cod — insisting it must be sold out — is a perfect collision of her world and reality
jokes.explanation: A beautiful encapsulation of Moira's relationship with reality. She cannot process that the café simply doesn't make miso black cod — the only explanation is that demand has outstripped supply. The joke works on first viewing and deepens as a character study. Twyla's straight 'We don't have that' is the perfect deadpan setup.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Did someone say fine dining? The Café Tropical has certainly been described as "fine."'
jokes.setup: Moira is promoting local dining in the tourism video
jokes.punchline: 'Has certainly been described as fine' — the passive construction ('been described as') avoids any personal endorsement, while 'fine' in quotation marks implies it's fine in the technical sense, not the superlative sense
jokes.explanation: Moira's refusal to actually endorse anything while technically promoting it is comedy gold. The passive voice ('been described as') is a legal hedge — she's not saying it's fine, someone else did — and 'fine' is the most damning possible adjective for a restaurant. Very sharp writing.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'll have the Miso Black Cod, please.' / Twyla: 'We don't have that.' / Moira: 'You mean, you're sold out, is more like it!'
jokes.setup: Moira greets Twyla at the Café for the tourism video
jokes.punchline: Moira orders an item that definitely isn't on the Café Tropical menu (Miso Black Cod), Twyla correctly says they don't have it, and Moira refuses to accept this reality — insisting it must be sold out
jokes.explanation: Moira's inability to accept that the Café Tropical doesn't serve Japanese-American fine dining is a perfect character joke. 'You mean, you're sold out, is more like it!' is the line — it's Moira refusing to let reality puncture her promotion, which simultaneously insults the restaurant and makes the tourism video actively misleading. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: Moira describing the Apothecary as having 'no noisy customers or busy cash registers here, to intrude upon your inner cogitations.'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to sell the Apothecary as a tourist destination in her video
jokes.punchline: She's accidentally describing the store as empty and unsuccessful — 'no noisy customers or busy cash registers' — as though this is a feature rather than a problem
jokes.explanation: Moira's tourism pitch accidentally becoming a litany of the town's failures — packaged as virtues — is a brilliantly sustained joke. The Apothecary being described as a place of peaceful emptiness (because no customers come) is simultaneously great tourism writing failure and great character comedy. 'Inner cogitations' is a wonderful Moira phrase.

jokes.text: David: 'It's-it's not usually this quiet.' / Moira: 'Oh, it's always nice and quiet here.' / David: 'Can you stop saying that?'
jokes.setup: Moira has described the Apothecary as having 'no noisy customers or busy cash registers'
jokes.punchline: David desperately trying to correct the record while Moira insists on its emptiness as a positive, both of them making things worse for the video
jokes.explanation: The interplay between David's panicked corrections and Moira's blithe insistence is great comedy of escalation. 'Can you stop saying that?' while the video is presumably still filming is exactly right.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And don't forget to say hello to your handsome proprietor, Mr. Johnathon Rose.' / Johnny (off-camera): 'John, no.'
jokes.setup: Moira is filming at the motel and introduces Johnny in her tourism video
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat off-camera 'John, no' — refusing to participate in whatever Moira is about to subject him to — is a perfect button delivered in two words
jokes.explanation: Two words, completely in character. Johnny has clearly been watching this video spectacle unfold and has decided not to participate. 'John, no' — refusing to be 'Johnathon' — is also a quiet correction of the name. The off-camera delivery is the right choice.

jokes.text: Moira's closing tagline: 'If heaven had a creek, it would be this one.'
jokes.setup: Moira has spent the entire video accidentally insulting the town while trying to promote it
jokes.punchline: Her closing line — 'if heaven had a creek, it would be this one' — is technically a compliment but the conditional 'if heaven had a creek' implies heaven might not have a creek, which undermines the whole thing. It's also oddly poetic in a way that sounds like Moira wrote it herself.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Moira closing line — technically a positive endorsement but structurally self-undermining. 'If heaven had a creek' introduces a premise that doesn't land as confident praise. The ambiguity is the joke. Also functions as a kind of meta-commentary on the show's title.

tmdb_overview: Johnny becomes overly involved in Alexis' romantic life, while Moira offends the townspeople in an interview with People magazine.
insight: David's deadpan reactions carry an 83-point episode built on character timing alone.
